  • Hide All Code

Money and Success in Football

Does Financial Strength Predict League Position? Sports Economics Seminar — Summer Semester 2026

Md. Muktadir

July 09, 2026

1 Executive Summary

Scale of the study: 976 team-seasons across 5 major European leagues (Bundesliga, La Liga, Ligue 1, Premier League, Serie A) covering 10 seasons (2015–16 to 2024–25). All leagues and seasons are included in the analysis.

Core finding: The Pearson correlation between Financial Rank and final league position is r = 0.813 (Spearman ρ = 0.814, both p < 0.001). The best-fitting model — Model H, which adds a quadratic (squared) term to Financial Rank — explains R² = 0.671 of all variation in final standings. In plain terms, knowing only how wealthy a club is relative to its peers allows us to correctly explain 67% of where they finish.

Champions by financial rank: 76% of the 50 league titles in the study were won by the squad with the highest market value in their league that season (Financial Rank = 1). Only 4 champions came from outside the financial top three — and only one of them (Leicester City 2015–16) qualifies as a genuine statistical outlier.

The one true exception: Leicester City 2015–16 finished Financial Rank 11 in the Premier League yet won the title. The model predicted they would finish 11th; instead they came 1st. Their residual of -10.5 positions is the largest shock in the entire dataset and a once-in-a-generation event.

Extended robustness (Step 7): the core finding is additionally stress-tested against a two-period time split, an alternative outcome variable (points instead of position), and a cross-sport comparison with the NBA’s salary-capped league. It survives all three — though the NBA comparison shows that a hard salary cap can meaningfully weaken, without eliminating, the link between spending and winning.

2 Introduction

2.1 Why Does This Question Matter?

The five major European football leagues — the English Premier League, Spanish La Liga, German Bundesliga, Italian Serie A, and French Ligue 1 — collectively generate over €18 billion in annual revenue, with squad transfer spending exceeding €12 billion in recent seasons. With this much money flowing through the sport, a natural and important question arises: does financial investment actually buy success, or can a well-organised, under-funded club systematically compete at the top level?

This question is not just about football. It has direct implications for:

  • Competitive balance policy — UEFA introduced Financial Fair Play precisely to prevent the wealthiest clubs from buying their way to dominance. If money truly determines outcomes, these policies become even more critical.
  • Fan engagement — If leagues are financially predetermined, the drama of competition is diminished for supporters of smaller clubs.
  • Sports economics theory — Understanding how resource endowments translate to tournament outcomes informs models across all competitive sports.

2.2 What We Set Out to Answer

This study addresses three interrelated questions using ten years of data from all five top-tier European leagues:

  1. Direction and strength: Is there a statistically significant relationship between a squad’s financial rank within its league and its final position? How strong is that relationship?
  2. Model form: Does a simple linear relationship capture the data, or is there a more complex (e.g., non-linear) pattern? Does the relationship differ across leagues or change over time?
  3. Exceptions: Which clubs defy the financial hierarchy, and how statistically unusual are they? Is Leicester City 2015–16 a miracle or merely a rounding error?

2.3 What This Study Adds

Previous research (Szymanski & Kuypers, 1999; Frick, 2007) has shown that wage bills and transfer spending predict league success. This study makes two additional contributions. First, instead of raw market values, we use a within-league financial rank — which club was the n-th wealthiest in their specific league that season. This removes the scale problem: a €300M squad is the richest in Ligue 1 but the fifth-richest in the Premier League. By ranking within leagues, we can pool all five leagues and make fair comparisons. Second, we introduce a residual-based outlier framework to systematically identify and quantify clubs that dramatically beat or missed their financial prediction.

3 The Data We Used

3.1 Where the Data Comes From

Our dataset was assembled by combining two sources:

  • ESPN FC provided final league standings (League Position) for all five leagues across the 2015–16 through 2024–25 seasons.
  • Transfermarkt provided squad market values — the estimated total transfer market worth of all players in each squad — at the start of each season.

After merging these two sources and removing any rows with missing values on key variables, we have a clean dataset of 976 team-season observations. Every team that played a full season in one of the five leagues during the study period appears once per season.

3.2 Descriptive Statistics

The table below summarises the key characteristics of the dataset, broken down by league and combined.

Sample size and position/market-value statistics by league.
Group N Mean Pos. SD Pos. Mean MV (M EUR) SD MV (M EUR)
ALL LEAGUES (combined) 976 10.28 5.66 277.7 254.3
Bundesliga 180 9.50 5.20 237.2 205.6
La Liga 200 10.50 5.78 256.7 269.4
Ligue 1 196 10.32 5.69 184.7 193.9
Premier League 200 10.50 5.78 458.0 303.5
Serie A 200 10.50 5.78 245.8 182.4
Correlation between Financial Rank and final League Position by league.
Group N Pearson r (FR vs Pos.) Spearman rho (FR vs Pos.)
ALL LEAGUES (combined) 976 0.813 0.814
Bundesliga 180 0.783 0.783
La Liga 200 0.787 0.787
Ligue 1 196 0.791 0.791
Premier League 200 0.801 0.801
Serie A 200 0.890 0.890

Reading Tables 1a and 1b: The “Combined” row (highlighted in blue) captures all 976 observations. Table 1a shows that market values vary substantially across leagues — the Premier League average squad value is considerably higher than Ligue 1, for example — but positions are naturally centred around mid-table in all leagues. Table 1b shows the key finding from raw data alone: the Pearson correlation between Financial Rank and final position is above 0.80 in every single league, and Serie A reaches 0.89. These numbers are striking — without running any model, the raw correlations already tell us money is a very powerful predictor of where clubs finish.

3.3 Variable Definitions

The table below defines each variable used in the analysis.

Variable definitions.
Variable Type Range Description
League_Position Integer 1–18/20 Final league standing; 1 = champion, higher number = worse finish
Financial_Rank (FR) Integer 1–18/20 Squad rank by market value within league-season; 1 = richest squad
Market_Value_M_EUR Numeric 10–3300+ Total squad market value in millions of euros (from Transfermarkt)
Normalized_Value Numeric 0–1 Market value normalised within league-season (min-max or value share)
Log_Normalized_Value Numeric −∞–0 Natural log of Normalized_Value; makes the skewed distribution more symmetric
Champion_bin Binary (0/1) 0 or 1 1 if the team won the league title that season, 0 otherwise
Season_Index Integer 1–10 Numeric season counter: 1 = 2015–16, 10 = 2024–25

The key design choice — Financial Rank instead of raw market value: Using Financial Rank (FR) rather than the raw €-value is crucial for a multi-league analysis. Consider: a squad worth €300M is the richest in Ligue 1 but only the fifth-richest in the Premier League. If we used raw €-values and pooled all leagues, we would be comparing apples to oranges. By ranking each squad within its own league-season, we create a fair, scale-free measure. FR = 1 always means “the wealthiest club in this league this season,” regardless of whether that squad is worth €600M or €150M.

3.4 How Financial Rank Is Distributed

The chart below shows the distribution of Financial Rank values across all five leagues. Since every league-season has exactly as many clubs as positions, the distribution should be near-uniform — and indeed it is. This confirms that the ranking system is clean and well-balanced across the dataset.

Distribution of Financial Rank across all leagues (2015-16 to 2024-25). The near-uniform distribution confirms that each rank value appears approximately equally often within every league-season.

4 Step 1 — First Look: Does Money Relate to Success?

4.1 The Main Scatter Plot

The most direct way to answer our research question is to plot Financial Rank against final League Position for all 976 team-seasons. The figure below does exactly that.

Financial Rank vs. Final League Position for all 975 team-seasons. Each dot is one team in one season, coloured by league. The dark fitted line (with shaded 95% confidence band) comes from Model H — our best-fitting quadratic model. The y-axis is reversed so that 1st place (champion) is at the top.

What this plot tells us: The downward-sloping pattern is immediately clear. Clubs with Financial Rank 1 or 2 cluster near the top of their league, while clubs with Financial Rank 15+ cluster near the bottom. The relationship is not perfect — there is considerable spread at every rank — but the trend is unmistakable. The dark fitted curve confirms this mathematically: as FR increases, predicted position gets worse, and the curve bends slightly at the bottom, suggesting the penalty for being the poorest club is slightly smaller than the reward for being the richest.

The Pearson correlation is r = 0.813 and the Spearman rank correlation is ρ = 0.814 (both statistically significant at p < 0.001, N = 976). A correlation above 0.8 is considered very strong in social science contexts, and here we achieve it with a single variable.

4.2 Is the Relationship the Same in Every League?

A legitimate concern is whether the relationship might exist in some leagues but not others. The chart below shows the Pearson correlation and R² (variance explained) for each league separately.

R-squared (variance explained in final position) by Financial Rank predictor, broken down by league. Financial Rank alone explains between 61% and 79% of final standings, with Serie A showing the strongest relationship.

League-level correlation and simple regression results.
League N Pearson r Spearman rho R2 (FR) beta(FR)
Bundesliga 180 0.783 0.783 0.613 0.783
La Liga 200 0.787 0.787 0.619 0.787
Ligue 1 196 0.791 0.791 0.625 0.791
Premier League 200 0.801 0.801 0.641 0.800
Serie A 200 0.890 0.890 0.792 0.890

What the league breakdown shows: Every single league displays a strong and statistically significant relationship between Financial Rank and final position. Serie A (Italian football) shows the highest correlation (r = 0.889, R² = 0.791), meaning that in Italy, where you rank financially almost perfectly determines where you finish. The Premier League (r = 0.801) and Bundesliga also show very strong relationships. Even the weakest correlation (Ligue 1, r ≈ 0.80) remains extremely strong by any standard. This rules out the possibility that the pooled result is driven by one unusual league — the financial hierarchy operates uniformly across all five leagues.

4.3 Relationship by League: Faceted View

The figure below shows the scatter of Financial Rank vs. League Position separately for each league. The gold band around each fitted line shows the 95% confidence interval. All five leagues show the same clear positive slope.

FR vs. Final League Position faceted by league, with individual linear fits. The slope and significance are consistent across all five leagues.

5 Step 2 — Building the Best Predictive Model

5.1 Our Strategy: Try Multiple Models, Compare, Pick the Best

A correlation tells us there is a relationship, but it does not give us a predictive model or tell us the precise form of the relationship. To go further, we fit a series of OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) regression models. The idea is to start simple and progressively add complexity, then compare the models formally to choose the best one.

The table below summarises the five model specifications we tested:

Step Question being asked Model
Baseline Does FR alone predict position? A: Position ~ FR
League FE Does adding league intercepts help? B: Position ~ FR + League
Alternative Is log(Market Value) better than FR? C: Position ~ Log(MV)
Full alternative Log(MV) + league + time controls D: Position ~ Log(MV) + League + Season
Non-linear Does a curved (quadratic) fit help? H: Position ~ FR + FR²

We also fit a logistic regression (Model E) to predict championship probability, which we discuss in Section 5.

Model selection uses AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) — a lower AIC means a better trade-off between fit and model complexity. We also report R² and adjusted R² for completeness, and verify our preferred model with formal F-tests.

5.2 Model Comparison Results

Model comparison. (*) = preferred specification (lowest AIC among OLS models).
Model Formula R² Adj. R² AIC BIC
A FR 0.6606 0.6602 5104.4 5119.1
B FR + League FE 0.6607 0.659 5112.0 5146.2
C Log(MV) 0.5331 0.5327 5415.5 5430.2
D Log(MV) + League + Season 0.6412 0.639 5168.5 5207.6
G FR x League 0.6624 0.6593 5115.1 5168.8
H FR + FR-sq (*) 0.6713 0.6706 5075.1 5094.7
H2 FR + FR-sq + League 0.6717 0.6697 5081.9 5120.9
H3 FR + FR-sq + Season 0.6713 0.6703 5077.1 5101.5
H4 FR + FR-sq + League + Season 0.6717 0.6693 5083.8 5127.8
I FR + FR-sq + FR-cub 0.6713 0.6703 5076.9 5101.4
J Log(FR) 0.6260 0.6256 5199.0 5213.6
M sqrt(FR) 0.6689 0.6685 5080.2 5094.9
E Champion ~ FR (Logit) 0.5478 — 182.4 —
Note:
OLS: League_Position (1=champion). Model E R² is McFadden pseudo-R²; Adj. R² and BIC undefined for logit (—). (*) Model H preferred; H2/H3/H4 have higher AIC.

Model H wins decisively. The highlighted row (Model H: League_Position = a + b1 x FR + b2 x FR-squared) achieves the lowest AIC of any OLS model — AIC = 5075.1. Compared to the simple linear baseline (Model A, AIC ≈ 5099.8), adding just one extra term (FR-squared) reduces AIC by 24.7 points.

Extended models H2, H3, and H4 (shaded grey) confirm the baseline. Model H2 adds league fixed effects (AIC = 5077.3), Model H3 adds a linear season trend (AIC = 5072.5), and Model H4 includes both controls (AIC = 5079.3) — all three are worse than Model H by AIC. A joint nested F-test comparing H4 to H yields F(5) = 0.247, p = 0.941, confirming that neither league dummies nor the season trend add explanatory power. Full details and coefficient tables are reported in Section 9.4.

Model I adds a cubic term but does not significantly improve fit over Model H (see Section 9.1). Models J and M are simpler one-term alternatives that also capture diminishing returns in a different mathematical form, but both produce higher AIC than Model H.

The quadratic specification is therefore statistically optimal among all tested forms and is theoretically motivated by the principle of diminishing returns to financial advantage at the top of the financial hierarchy.

5.3 What Does Model H Actually Say? (The Coefficients)

Having selected Model H as our best model, we now interpret its estimated coefficients in detail.

Model H coefficient estimates. Response: League Position (1 = champion).
Term Estimate Std. Error t-value p-value 95% CI
Intercept (α) 0.4172 0.3441 1.212 2.26e-01 [-0.2573, 1.0916]
Financial Rank (b1) 1.2309 0.0765 16.093 7.65e-52 [1.0810, 1.3808]
FR-squared (b2) -0.0202 0.0036 -5.633 2.32e-08 [-0.0273, -0.0132]

Understanding the three coefficients in plain language:

  • Intercept (α ≈ −0.38): This is the predicted position for a hypothetical club with FR = 0 (i.e., before we account for their rank). It has no direct real-world interpretation but anchors the curve.

  • β₁ ≈ 1.231 (Financial Rank): For every one step increase in Financial Rank — moving from the 1st to the 2nd wealthiest club, for example — the predicted final position gets worse by roughly 1.23 places, all else being equal. This is the core result: being poorer than your rivals costs you about 1.2 league positions per rank step at the top of the financial hierarchy.

  • β₂ ≈ −0.020 (Financial Rank²): The negative quadratic coefficient means diminishing returns at the bottom of the table. As FR increases, the marginal positional penalty for each additional rank step shrinks. At FR = 1, the marginal cost of dropping one rank is approximately 1.23 − 2 × 0.020 × 1 = 1.19 positions. At FR = 15, the same step only costs 1.23 − 2 × 0.020 × 15 = 0.63 positions. In practical terms: the gap between the richest and second-richest club in a league is larger than the gap between the 14th and 15th richest.

  • R² = 0.671: Together, the linear and quadratic FR terms explain 67.1% of the total variation in final league position across all 975 team-seasons — using just one predictor variable (FR, in two forms).

5.4 Visualising the Quadratic Fit

The curve below makes the non-linear relationship concrete. Notice that the predicted position drops steeply as FR moves from 1 to 5 (the top of the financial hierarchy), and more gradually from FR 10 to FR 20 (the bottom).

Model H predicted league position as a function of Financial Rank, with 95% confidence interval. The y-axis is reversed: lower position number = better finish. Key predicted values are annotated at FR = 1, 5, 10, 15, 20.

Reading the curve: A club ranked 1st financially is predicted to finish around 2nd place. A club ranked 5th financially is predicted to finish around 5th–6th. By FR = 10, the model predicts a mid-table finish of around 11th–12th. At FR = 20 (the poorest club in a 20-team league), the predicted finish is around 16th–17th, not 20th — this is the “diminishing returns” effect: even the poorest club is not predicted to finish stone last, because their competitors at FR 18–20 are similarly constrained.

5.5 How Well Does FR Explain Each League Separately?

R-squared for Financial Rank (blue) and Log(Market Value) (green) as predictors, by league. In every league, Financial Rank explains more variance than Log(Market Value). Serie A has the highest explanatory power at 79%.

The chart confirms what Table 3 told us: Financial Rank explains 61–79% of final league position within each individual league, not just in the pooled analysis. Serie A shows the strongest predictability (R² = 0.79), while Ligue 1 and Bundesliga show the lowest (R² ≈ 0.61–0.64) — though even these are very high by social science standards.

6 Step 3 — Can We Predict Who Wins the Title?

6.1 A Different Question: Championship Probability

So far we have treated League Position as a continuous number and asked how Financial Rank shifts that number. But we can also ask a yes/no question: does Financial Rank predict whether a team wins the league at all? For this we use logistic regression, where the outcome is a binary variable — 1 if the team won the title, 0 otherwise (Model E).

The logistic model estimates the log-odds of winning the title as a linear function of Financial Rank:

\[\log\!\left(\frac{P(\text{Champion})}{1 - P(\text{Champion})}\right) = \alpha + \beta_{\text{FR}} \cdot \text{FR}\]

Here, a negative β means higher FR (poorer squad) reduces the odds of winning.

Table 6. Logistic Model E coefficients. McFadden R² = 0.548, AIC = 182.4.
Term Log-Odds Std. Error z-value p-value Odds Ratio
Intercept (α) 1.642 0.4033 4.070 4.70e-05 5.1636
Financial Rank (beta) -1.298 0.1900 -6.834 8.24e-12 0.2729

Interpreting the key coefficient: The estimated β = -1.298 means that each one-step increase in Financial Rank multiplies the odds of winning the title by e^(-1.298) = 0.273. That is a 72.7% reduction in championship odds for every additional rank step. Moving from FR 1 to FR 2 cuts championship odds by 72.7%; moving from FR 1 to FR 3 cuts it by nearly 92.6%.

6.2 The Championship Probability Curve

The figure below converts those log-odds into probabilities and plots them for every possible Financial Rank value.

Predicted probability of winning the league title as a function of Financial Rank (Logistic Model E). Key probability values are annotated at FR = 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, and 11.

Predicted championship probabilities at key FR values (Model E).
Financial Rank P(Champion)
1 58.5%
2 27.78%
3 9.5%
4 2.79%
5 0.78%
8 0.02%
11 0%

What this means in practice: The richest club in a league (FR = 1) has a predicted championship probability of 58.5% according to the model. That may sound low, but remember there are typically 18–20 clubs in a league, so the baseline probability of any random club winning is only 5–6%. An FR = 1 club is roughly 12 times more likely to win the title than an average club. By FR = 5, the probability drops to under 5%. By FR = 10 or higher, championship probability is essentially zero — which makes Leicester’s actual title in 2015–16 all the more remarkable.

Important caveat: Even the wealthiest squad only has a ~58.5% championship probability. Football is inherently random — injuries, fixture schedules, weather, individual brilliance, and managerial decisions all introduce variance. The model captures the structural advantage of financial strength but cannot account for the enormous randomness of any individual season.

7 Step 4 — Who Beat the Model? Residual Analysis

7.1 What Are Residuals and Why Do They Matter?

After fitting a model, the residual for each team-season is simply the difference between where they actually finished and where the model predicted they would finish:

Residual = Actual Position − Predicted Position

  • A negative residual means the team finished better than their finances predicted — they are overperformers.
  • A positive residual means the team finished worse than their finances predicted — they are underperformers.
  • A residual near zero means the model predicted them correctly.

We define a statistical threshold of ±1.5 standard deviations of the residual distribution. Teams beyond this threshold are flagged as genuine statistical outliers — clubs that are truly surprising relative to their finances.

7.2 The Residuals Plot

Residuals vs. Fitted Values for Model H. Each point is one team-season. Blue points are overperformers (finished better than predicted), red points are underperformers, grey points are within the normal range. The dashed lines mark the ±1.5 SD outlier thresholds. Notable clubs are labelled.

Reading this plot: The horizontal axis shows what the model predicted for each team (their expected league position based on FR). The vertical axis shows the residual. Most of the 976 observations cluster tightly around zero — the model predicts them well. A small number of points, however, fall far from zero. Among the overperformers (negative residuals, blue), Leicester City 2015–16 stands out dramatically at the bottom of the chart. Among the underperformers (positive residuals, red), clubs that spent heavily but underachieved appear at the top.

The residual standard deviation is 3.25 positions. Most clubs (approximately 845 of 976) fall within ±1.5 SD = ±4.9 positions of their prediction. Only 62 teams qualify as overperformers and 69 as underperformers by this threshold.

7.3 Cook’s Distance: Measuring Statistical Influence

Cook’s Distance measures how much the entire set of regression coefficients would change if a particular observation were removed. A high Cook’s Distance means that observation has a disproportionate pull on the model — it is statistically influential. The conventional threshold is 4/N = 0.0041.

Cook’s Distance for all 975 observations. The dashed red line marks the threshold 4/N = 0.0041. Points above the line are statistically influential observations. The top 12 are labelled by club name and season.

What this tells us: There are 30 observations above the Cook’s Distance threshold. The most influential observation — by a wide margin — is Leicester City 2015–16. This is not a surprise: a club that finished 10 places better than the model predicted will strongly pull the fitted curve away from its optimal position. The key point is that Leicester is not just a dramatic story — it is a statistically influential data point that we need to acknowledge and understand.

7.4 Outlier Summary Tables by League

The 131 statistical outliers (teams whose residual exceeded ±1.5 SD = 4.87 positions) are presented below as one table per league, sorted within each league from largest over-performance (most negative residual) to largest under-performance. Blue rows = overperformers; red rows = underperformers.

7.4.4 Premier League

Table 8d. Premier League outliers (|Residual| > 4.87): 13 overperformer(s) [blue], 18 underperformer(s) [red].
Team Season Actual Pos. FR Predicted Residual
Leicester City 2015-2016 1 11 11.51 -10.51
Sheffield United 2019-2020 9 20 16.94 -7.94
Burnley 2017-2018 7 15 14.32 -7.32
West Bromwich Albion 2016-2017 10 19 16.49 -6.49
Fulham 2022-2023 10 19 16.49 -6.49
AFC Bournemouth 2016-2017 9 17 15.49 -6.49
Burnley 2019-2020 10 18 16.01 -6.01
Leeds United 2020-2021 9 16 14.93 -5.93
Brentford 2022-2023 9 16 14.93 -5.93
West Ham United 2020-2021 6 11 11.51 -5.51
Wolverhampton Wanderers 2018-2019 7 12 12.27 -5.27
Nottingham Forest 2024-2025 7 12 12.27 -5.27
Crystal Palace 2022-2023 11 18 16.01 -5.01
Aston Villa 2021-2022 14 8 8.97 5.03
Aston Villa 2015-2016 20 16 14.93 5.07
Sunderland 2016-2017 20 16 14.93 5.07
West Bromwich Albion 2017-2018 20 16 14.93 5.07
West Ham United 2019-2020 16 10 10.70 5.30
Stoke City 2017-2018 19 14 13.68 5.32
Fulham 2018-2019 19 14 13.68 5.32
Everton 2021-2022 16 9 9.85 6.15
Nottingham Forest 2023-2024 17 10 10.70 6.30
AFC Bournemouth 2019-2020 18 11 11.51 6.49
Leicester City 2022-2023 18 11 11.51 6.49
Southampton 2022-2023 20 13 13.00 7.00
Southampton 2017-2018 17 9 9.85 7.15
Newcastle United 2015-2016 18 10 10.70 7.30
Chelsea 2022-2023 12 3 3.93 8.07
Chelsea 2015-2016 10 1 1.63 8.37
Manchester United 2024-2025 15 5 6.07 8.93
Tottenham Hotspur 2024-2025 17 6 7.07 9.93

7.4.5 Serie A

Table 8e. Serie A outliers (|Residual| > 4.87): 7 overperformer(s) [blue], 7 underperformer(s) [red].
Team Season Actual Pos. FR Predicted Residual
Chievo Verona 2015-2016 9 18 16.01 -7.01
Hellas Verona 2019-2020 9 17 15.49 -6.49
Lazio 2022-2023 2 7 8.04 -6.04
Atalanta 2019-2020 3 8 8.97 -5.97
Sampdoria 2020-2021 9 16 14.93 -5.93
Atalanta 2016-2017 4 9 9.85 -5.85
Napoli 2024-2025 1 5 6.07 -5.07
Cagliari 2020-2021 16 10 10.70 5.30
Udinese 2015-2016 17 11 11.51 5.49
Torino 2020-2021 17 11 11.51 5.49
Parma 2020-2021 20 15 14.32 5.68
Sampdoria 2015-2016 15 8 8.97 6.03
Napoli 2023-2024 10 3 3.93 6.07
Fiorentina 2018-2019 16 7 8.04 7.96

Reading the tables: Within each league, clubs are sorted from the greatest over-performance (top of table) to the greatest under-performance (bottom). A negative residual means the club finished better than their Financial Rank predicted; a positive residual means they finished worse. Leicester City 2015–16 leads all leagues with a residual of -10.5 positions — the most extreme over-performance in the entire dataset.

8 Step 5 — The Exceptions: Cinderella Stories

8.1 Champions from Outside the Financial Top Three

Over 10 seasons across 5 leagues, there were 50 league champions in total. Of these, 76% (38 clubs) were the financially wealthiest club in their league that season. An additional cluster came from FR = 2 and FR = 3. But how many champions came from outside the financial top three?

Table 9. The 4 champions who won from outside the financial top 3 (FR > 3).
League Season Club FR Squad Value (M€) Model Prediction Residual
Premier League 2015-2016 Leicester City 11 161.1 11.5 -10.5
Serie A 2024-2025 Napoli 5 500.4 6.1 -5.1
Ligue 1 2020-2021 Lille 4 323.2 5.0 -4.0
Premier League 2024-2025 Liverpool 4 951.0 5.0 -4.0

The sobering reality: Only 4 of 50 champions (≈ 8%) came from outside the financial top three. Of these 4 cases, three clubs (Napoli FR = 5, Lille FR = 4, Liverpool FR = 4) were financially competitive — only one step or two outside the top tier, and within one standard deviation of expectation. Only Leicester City (FR = 11) is a genuine statistical outlier. The rest of the “surprises” are barely surprising at all when you examine their finances closely.

8.2 The Distribution of Champion Financial Ranks

Distribution of Financial Rank among all 50 league champions across 10 seasons and 5 leagues. Blue bars show the ‘expected zone’ (FR 1-3); red bars show the ‘surprise zone’ (FR 4+). The dominance of FR 1 is striking.

What this chart shows: The vast majority of league titles go to clubs ranked 1st, 2nd, or 3rd financially. The bar for FR = 1 alone towers over the others — 76% of all championships. As we move to FR 4 and beyond, the bars shrink rapidly toward zero. This is a visual summary of the study’s core finding: the financial hierarchy translates almost directly into the sporting hierarchy.

8.3 All 50 Champions: Ranked by How Much They Beat the Model

Champions residuals vs. Model H prediction, split by league (10 champions each). Each mini-panel shows how much better each title winner finished relative to what their Financial Rank predicted. Blue shading = outlier zone (< -1.5 SD). Diamond = surprise winner (FR > 3). All 50 champions have a negative residual — every title required beating the financial prediction.

A fascinating observation: Every single one of the 50 champions has a negative residual — meaning every champion finished better than their finances alone would predict. This makes intuitive sense: winning a title requires exceptional performance, not just adequate resources. Even the richest clubs often finish 2nd or 3rd despite being predicted to win. What the chart makes clear is that for most champions, the overperformance is modest (a few positions). For Leicester, it is off the chart.

8.4 Leicester City 2015–16: A Closer Look

Leicester City’s Premier League title in 2015–16 is widely regarded as one of the greatest upsets in sporting history. Our data allows us to quantify exactly how unusual it was.

Premier League team-seasons plotted by Financial Rank vs. Final Position. All observations except Leicester 2015-16 are shown in blue; Leicester is highlighted in red. The model-fitted curve (dark line) shows what was expected. Leicester’s position is far outside the normal pattern.

Leicester City 2015-16 vs. model expectations.
Metric Value
Financial Rank (Premier League, 2015–16) 11th (out of 20)
Squad Market Value €161M
League Average Market Value €243M (Premier League avg.)
Model H: Predicted Position 11.5
Actual Final Position 1st (Champions)
Residual -10.5 positions
Residuals SD (all 975 obs.) 3.25
Residual in SD units -3.2 SD
Cook’s Distance 0.0079
Cook’s D Threshold (4/N) 0.0041

Quantifying the miracle: Leicester’s residual of -10.5 positions corresponds to -3.2 standard deviations below the mean. In a normal distribution, an observation 3.2 SDs from the mean occurs with probability well below 0.1%. Their Cook’s Distance is the highest in the dataset, confirming it is not a data error but a genuine, mathematically unprecedented event in this 10-season study. Importantly, removing Leicester from the dataset does not weaken our core finding — if anything, it marginally increases Model H’s R², showing the result is robust to this extraordinary case.

9 Step 6 — Is This Finding Reliable? Robustness Checks

9.1 Confirming the Quadratic Shape with a Formal F-Test

We confirmed that the non-linear (quadratic) specification is genuinely better than a linear model using a nested F-test. This test asks: does adding FR² significantly improve fit over Model A (FR alone)?

Nested F-test results for Model H specification checks.
Comparison df F-statistic p-value Verdict
Interaction (FR*League) vs Additive (FR+League) 4 1.202 0.3082 Effect is league-uniform
Quadratic (FR+FR^2) vs Linear (FR) 1 31.726 < 0.001 *** Quadratic term confirmed
Time-trend (FR*Season) vs Additive (FR+Season) 1 0.419 0.5174 Effect is time-stable
Cubic (FR+FR2+FR3) vs Quadratic (FR+FR^2) 1 0.184 0.6679 Cubic term not needed — Model H sufficient

All four robustness checks pass: (1) The quadratic term FR² is highly significant (F = 31.71, p < 0.001), confirming the non-linear shape is real and not a coincidence. (2) The interaction of FR with League is not significant, confirming the effect is the same in all five leagues — the financial hierarchy works the same way in Italy as in England. (3) There is no significant time trend (FR × Season interaction), confirming the relationship has been stable from 2015–16 through 2024–25. (4) The cubic term FR³ is not significant (F = 0.20, p = 0.653), confirming that the quadratic specification (Model H: League_Position = a + b1 x FR + b2 x FR-squared) is sufficient and adding higher-order polynomial terms does not improve fit.

9.2 Addressing Heteroskedasticity

A potential concern with OLS models is heteroskedasticity — when the variance of the residuals is not constant across fitted values. If present, the standard errors we report may be unreliable.

Diagnostic tests for heteroskedasticity and autocorrelation.
Test Statistic p-value Implication
Breusch-Pagan (heteroskedasticity) 45.52 < 0.001 *** Residual variance is not constant — robust standard errors recommended
Durbin-Watson (autocorrelation) 1.18 < 0.001 *** Observations within a team correlated across seasons

The Breusch–Pagan test detects significant heteroskedasticity (p < 0.001). To verify our results are not affected, we re-estimated the model using HC1 heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors and compared them to the regular OLS standard errors.

Model D: OLS vs. HC1 robust standard errors. The main predictor remains highly significant.
Term Estimate OLS SE HC1 Robust SE % Change
(Intercept) -0.2152 0.4049 0.3712 -8.3
Log_Normalized_Value -5.9663 0.1439 0.1227 -14.7
League: La Liga -0.2012 0.3507 0.3547 1.2
League: Ligue 1 -1.1028 0.3542 0.3587 1.3
League: Premier League: 4.0859 0.3573 0.3533 -1.1
League: Serie A 2.7888 0.3521 0.3119 -11.4
Season_Index 0.1059 0.0381 0.0387 1.5

The changes in standard errors are modest. The main predictor (Log(Market Value)) remains highly significant under robust standard errors (z = 48.5, p < 0.001). This confirms our core finding is not sensitive to heteroskedasticity.

9.3 Time Stability: Has the Relationship Changed Over a Decade?

A natural question is whether the financial-to-position relationship has grown stronger or weaker over the 10 seasons studied — perhaps due to increasing financial inequality (the “super-league effect”) or improvements in analytics that help smaller clubs compete.

Time-trend coefficients: FR × Season Index interaction.
Term Estimate SE t-value p-value
Season_Index -0.0474 0.0765 -0.6196 0.5357
Financial_Rank:Season_Index 0.0042 0.0065 0.6476 0.5174

The estimated effect of Financial Rank on League Position plotted across all 10 seasons. The flat trend line and wide confidence intervals confirm there is no significant time trend — the relationship has not changed from 2015-16 to 2024-25.

The interaction coefficient FR × Season_Index is very close to zero and statistically non-significant (p > 0.05). The financial-position relationship has neither strengthened nor weakened over the decade studied. It was as powerful in the 2024–25 season as it was in 2015–16. Financial inequality has not increased (or decreased) in its predictive power over this window.

9.4 Model H Extended: Adding League and Season Controls

A natural follow-up question is whether Model H’s fit improves if we explicitly add league fixed effects (to absorb cross-league heterogeneity in average positions) and a linear time trend (Season_Index, to absorb any secular drift). We fit three extended variants built on Model H’s quadratic FR specification:

  • Model H2: League_Position ~ FR + FR-squared + League (4 league dummy variables)
  • Model H3: League_Position ~ FR + FR-squared + Season_Index (linear time trend only)
  • Model H4: League_Position ~ FR + FR-squared + League + Season_Index (both controls)

All three are compared to the baseline Model H via AIC and formal nested F-tests.

Table E1. Model H family comparison: adding League and Season controls.
Model Formula N R² Adj. R² AIC BIC
H FR + FR² 976 0.6713 0.6706 5075 5095
H2 FR + FR² + League 976 0.6717 0.6697 5082 5121
H3 FR + FR² + Season 976 0.6713 0.6703 5077 5102
H4 FR + FR² + League + Season 976 0.6717 0.6693 5084 5128
Table E2. Nested F-tests: extending Model H with League and Season controls.
Comparison df F-statistic p-value
H2 vs H: Adding League FE 4 0.309 0.8721
H3 vs H: Adding Season_Index 1 0.028 0.8660
H4 vs H: Adding League + Season (joint) 5 0.253 0.9385
H4 vs H2: Season beyond League 1 0.029 0.8655
H4 vs H3: League beyond Season 4 0.309 0.8722
Table E3. Model H4 full coefficient table (quadratic FR + League fixed effects + Season trend).
Term Estimate Std. Error t-value p-value
(Intercept) 0.2022 0.4583 0.4411 0.6592
Financial_Rank 1.2358 0.0768 16.0997 < 0.001 ***
I(Financial_Rank^2) -0.0206 0.0036 -5.6903 < 0.001 ***
LeagueLa Liga 0.3091 0.3356 0.9209 0.3573
LeagueLigue 1 0.2523 0.3369 0.7491 0.4540
LeaguePremier League 0.3111 0.3356 0.9270 0.3542
LeagueSerie A 0.3055 0.3356 0.9102 0.3630
Season_Index -0.0062 0.0364 -0.1694 0.8655

What the results show: Model H4 achieves R² = 0.6714 and AIC = 5079.27, compared to Model H’s R² = 0.6709 and AIC = 5070.52. The nested F-test comparing H4 to Model H yields F(5) = 0.247, p = 0.941, which is not significant at p < 0.05 — Model H wins on AIC by 8.75 points. Neither the League fixed effects (F(4) = 0.304, p = 0.876) nor the Season_Index term (F(1) = 0.023, p = 0.880) add significant explanatory power beyond the quadratic baseline. The Season_Index coefficient in H4 is −0.006 (SE = 0.036, p = 0.879), confirming no statistically meaningful time trend. None of the four League fixed effects are statistically significant (all p > 0.35). Conclusion: Model H (FR + FR-squared) is the correct and sufficient specification. Adding league intercepts or a time control does not improve fit — the quadratic Financial Rank model generalises equally well across all five leagues and all ten seasons without further adjustment.

10 Step 7 — Extended Robustness and External Validity

The robustness checks in Step 6 all use the same outcome variable (final league position) and the same pooled dataset. This section pushes further along three independent axes: a cleaner, more intuitive time-split test; a completely different outcome variable (points instead of position); and a completely different sport (the NBA) with a fundamentally different financial structure — a hard salary cap. If Model H’s finding is genuinely robust, it should survive all three.

10.1 A Cleaner Two-Period Robustness Check

Step 6 already showed that a continuous FR × Season_Index interaction is not significant. Here we repeat the idea in the plainest possible way: split the 10 seasons into two five-season halves — Early (2015–16 to 2019–20) and Late (2020–21 to 2024–25) — and re-fit Model H completely independently on each half. If financial inequality has been increasing (a “super-league effect”) or shrinking (better analytics helping smaller clubs), the fitted curve should visibly differ between the two halves.

Model H fit separately on the Early (2015–16 to 2019–20) and Late (2020–21 to 2024–25) halves of the decade.
Period N R² Adj. R² AIC FR slope 95% CI low 95% CI high FR² slope
Early (2015-16 to 2019-20) 490 0.6463 0.6448 2591 1.25 1.029 1.470 -0.0219
Late (2020-21 to 2024-25) 486 0.6975 0.6962 2487 1.21 1.006 1.415 -0.0185

Financial Rank vs. League Position, fit separately for each five-season half. The two quadratic curves are visually almost indistinguishable.

Financial Rank slope with 95% confidence interval, Early vs. Late period. The intervals overlap substantially.

The Early-period FR slope is 1.2497 (95% CI [1.0292, 1.4702]); the Late-period slope is 1.2102 (95% CI [1.0056, 1.4147]) — the intervals overlap almost completely. A formal interaction test (Financial_Rank × Period, fit on the pooled data) confirms this: the interaction coefficient is -0.0395 and not statistically significant (p = 0.797).

Independent confirmation of time stability. Two entirely different test designs — a continuous FR × Season_Index interaction (Step 6) and a binary Early/Late split with its own interaction test (here) — reach the same conclusion: the financial-to-position relationship has not measurably changed between the first and second half of the decade studied.

10.2 Robustness to the Outcome Measure: Points Instead of Position

Final league position is a rank, not a raw quantity — a club that misses out on the title by one point looks identical, position-wise, to one that misses out by twenty. Points are a continuous measure of season-long dominance and provide an independent check: does the same financial hierarchy show up when we stop compressing performance into a 1–20 ranking?

Model H re-fit with Points as the outcome: Points ~ FR + FR-squared.
Term Estimate Std. Error t-value p-value
(Intercept) 85.1162 1.0403 81.82 < 0.001 ***
Financial_Rank -5.0466 0.2312 -21.83 < 0.001 ***
I(Financial_Rank^2) 0.1268 0.0109 11.67 < 0.001 ***

As expected, the sign flips relative to the position model: because higher points are better (unlike position, where 1st is best), the Financial_Rank coefficient is now negative — each step down the financial hierarchy costs a club points, not positions. The model explains R² = 0.678 of the variation in points, comparable to the R² = 0.671 obtained with position as the outcome.

Financial Rank vs. Points won that season, with the quadratic fit overlaid. Gold diamonds mark the three key story clubs from Steps 4–5.

Does the outlier ranking change? We recomputed residuals from this points-based model and re-ranked every team-season by absolute residual size.

Top 10 largest |residual| under the points-based model, alongside each team’s rank in the original position-based model.
Team Season Fin. Rank Points Points Residual Rank (position model)
Leicester City 2015-2016 11 81 36.1 3
Lyon 2019-2020 2 40 -35.5 185
AS Monaco 2018-2019 3 36 -35.1 1
Lens 2022-2023 8 84 31.1 38
AS Monaco 2019-2020 3 40 -31.1 114
Chelsea 2015-2016 1 50 -30.2 14
Bayer Leverkusen 2016-2017 3 41 -30.1 15
Chelsea 2022-2023 3 44 -27.1 16
VfL Wolfsburg 2015-2016 3 45 -26.1 195
VfL Wolfsburg 2016-2017 5 37 -26.1 4

Leicester City 2015–16 still has the single largest residual — with points as the outcome, exactly as with position. This is a reassuring confirmation: the “greatest statistical upset” finding from Step 5 is not an artefact of using position rather than points.

What does change is which clubs round out the rest of the list. Where the position-based residuals (Step 4) foreground overperforming underdogs, the points-based residuals surface a complementary story: rich clubs that collapsed. AS Monaco (2018–19 and 2019–20), Chelsea (2015–16 and 2022–23), Bayer Leverkusen (2016–17), and VfL Wolfsburg (2015–16 and 2016–17) all appear among the ten largest points residuals — all financially top-5 clubs that won far fewer points than their wealth predicted. Chelsea’s back-to-back collapses (defending champions finishing mid-table in 2015–16, then a 12th-place finish in 2022–23 despite record transfer spending under new ownership) and AS Monaco’s post-2017 financial retrenchment are well-known real-world stories that this purely statistical residual ranking recovers without being told to look for them. Lens (2022–23, Financial Rank 8) is the only genuinely new positive surprise that does not already appear among the position-model’s largest residuals.

Robust to outcome measure. Whether success is measured by final position or by points won, Financial Rank predicts outcomes with comparable strength, and the single largest statistical anomaly in the dataset — Leicester City 2015–16 — is identified either way.

10.3 External Validity: Does Money Buy Success Outside Football?

Every check so far uses European football data. A stronger test of “does money buy success” is whether the same statistical pattern appears in a completely different sports league — ideally one with a structurally different financial system. The NBA is a natural candidate: unlike Europe’s football leagues, it operates under a hard salary cap, deliberately designed (like UEFA’s Financial Fair Play, mentioned in the Introduction) to limit financial dominance. If money still predicts finish as strongly in a capped league as in an uncapped one, that would suggest caps do little to level the field; if the relationship is much weaker, that is evidence the cap is doing its job.

We collected NBA team payroll and final standings for the same ten seasons studied for football (2015–16 to 2024–25), using ESPN’s standings API (final regular-season record, pooled across both conferences and ranked 1–30 by win percentage) and Spotrac’s historical team salary-cap tables (ranked 1–30 by total cap allocations, 1 = highest payroll). We then fit the identical Model H specification: Final_Standing ~ Payroll_Rank + Payroll_Rank².

A definitional caveat. Unlike European football, where the league table winner is the champion, the NBA crowns its champion through a separate playoff tournament — the best regular-season team frequently does not win the title (Oklahoma City Thunder’s 2024–25 championship, for example, came with a payroll rank of only 23rd out of 30). Final_Standing below always refers to the regular-season finish (directly comparable to football’s League_Position), while “champion” in the summary table below refers to the actual NBA Finals winner each season — a stricter, more literal reading of “champion” than simply topping the table.

Football vs. NBA: strength of the money-predicts-finish relationship, 2015–16 to 2024–25.
Sport N Pearson r R² AIC Unique Champions % Titles: Top-3 Payroll
Football (Top-5 European Leagues) 976 0.8127 0.6713 5075 16 92
NBA 300 0.4364 0.2027 2092 8 50

Side-by-side comparison: Financial Rank vs. League Position (European football, no salary cap) and Payroll Rank vs. Final Standing (NBA, hard salary cap). Both panels share the same quadratic specification and axis convention (rank 1 at the top).

The difference is stark. In football, Financial Rank explains R² = 0.671 of final position (r = 0.813, N = 975). In the NBA, Payroll Rank explains only R² = 0.203 of final standing (r = 0.436, N = 300) — a much noisier relationship, visible directly in the right-hand scatter of the figure above, where high-payroll and low-payroll teams are far more intermixed across the standings. The champion-level statistic tells the same story from a different angle: 92% of European titles went to a financial top-3 club, versus only 50% of NBA titles going to a top-3 payroll team (5 of 10, including the Thunder’s 2024–25 title won with the league’s 23rd-highest payroll — a result that would be a Leicester-level statistical outlier in the football data, but is a comparatively routine occurrence in the NBA).

Money predicts success everywhere, but the salary cap visibly weakens the relationship. The core “money buys success” pattern is not unique to European football — it re-appears in the NBA — but the strength of that pattern is far from universal. A league office can meaningfully weaken the link between spending and winning through plan design (a hard cap), even though it cannot eliminate it entirely. This is indirect but genuine evidence in favour of the logic behind mechanisms like UEFA’s Financial Fair Play, cited as motivation in the Introduction.

Limits of this comparison. The NBA dataset is much smaller (300 vs. 975 team-seasons; only 10 champion-seasons vs. 50), so the champion-level percentages in particular should be read with caution. This is also a single alternative sport with numerous other structural differences from football (playoff-determined titles, a fixed 30-team league, no promotion/relegation, different broadcast and roster-size economics) — any of which, not only the salary cap, could contribute to the weaker relationship observed.

11 Conclusions

11.1 What We Found: A Summary

This study has systematically examined whether financial strength predicts sporting success in Europe’s five major football leagues over 10 seasons, and then stress-tested that answer against alternative time periods, an alternative outcome measure, and an entirely different sport. The answer is unambiguous: yes, and very strongly so. Here are the six main findings:

Finding 1 — Money very strongly predicts where clubs finish. The Pearson correlation between Financial Rank and final league position is r = 0.813 (Spearman ρ = 0.814), both statistically significant at p < 0.001 across all 976 team-seasons. The best-fitting model (Model H, quadratic in FR) explains R² = 0.671 — nearly two-thirds of all variation in final standings — using nothing but a club’s within-league wealth rank.

Finding 2 — The relationship is non-linear: diminishing returns at the bottom. Adding a squared term (FR²) significantly improves model fit (F = 31.71, p < 0.001). The marginal positional penalty of dropping one rank is largest at the top of the financial hierarchy (~1.19 positions per rank at FR 1→2) and shrinks toward the bottom (~0.63 positions per rank at FR 15→16). In plain language: the gap between the richest and second-richest club is much larger than the gap between the 15th and 16th richest.

Finding 3 — The effect is the same in every league and has not changed over time. The FR × League interaction is not significant (F = 1.17, p = 0.32), meaning the financial hierarchy operates identically in Germany, Spain, France, England, and Italy. The FR × Season time trend is also not significant (F = 0.44, p = 0.51), meaning the relationship has been equally strong throughout 2015–16 to 2024–25. Financial dominance is a universal and stable feature of European football, not a recent trend or a league-specific phenomenon.

Finding 4 — Exceptions exist, but only one is truly extraordinary. Of 50 league titles across the decade, only 4 were won by clubs outside the financial top three. Three of those four (Napoli, Lille, and Liverpool) were at FR 4–5, barely outside the expected zone, and within one standard deviation of expectation. Only Leicester City 2015–16 — Financial Rank 11, predicted to finish 11th, actually won the title — is a genuine statistical outlier (-3.2 SDs from the mean, highest Cook’s D in the dataset). Such events are statistically exceptional and cannot be used to argue that money does not matter.

Finding 5 — The result survives a battery of independent robustness checks. Splitting the decade into two independent five-season halves (above) yields statistically indistinguishable Financial Rank slopes (interaction p = 0.797); re-running Model H with points instead of position as the outcome (above) still identifies Leicester City 2015–16 as the single largest statistical anomaly, while additionally surfacing a complementary story of financially strong clubs (Chelsea, AS Monaco, Bayer Leverkusen) that badly underspent their points total relative to expectation. Neither check weakens the core finding.

Finding 6 — The pattern generalises beyond football, but a salary cap visibly weakens it. The same quadratic specification fit on ten seasons of NBA data (above) confirms that money-predicts-finish is not football-specific: Payroll Rank significantly predicts NBA final standing. But the relationship is far weaker under the NBA’s hard salary cap (R² = 0.203, r = 0.436) than in football’s unrestricted spending environment (R² = 0.671, r = 0.813), and only half of NBA titles in the period went to a top-3 payroll team, versus 92% in football. This is indirect evidence that deliberate cap-style mechanisms can meaningfully loosen — without eliminating — the link between spending and winning.

11.2 Limitations and Future Directions

Every study has limitations, and being transparent about them is part of good scientific practice. The table below summarises the main limitations and suggests how future research could address them.

Study limitations and suggested future extensions.
Limitation Issue Potential Fix
Market value as quality proxy Transfermarkt valuations may lag actual squad quality by weeks; subjective editor estimates Use wage bill data as an alternative or cross-validate with Opta squad ratings
No managerial quality variable Exceptional coaches (Ranieri at Leicester) are not captured in the financial model Add manager tenure, prior record, or a coaching quality index
No injury data Injury crises can dramatically alter a squad’s effective quality mid-season Incorporate injury-adjusted squad value (e.g., weighted by availability %)
Panel structure ignored Teams appear multiple seasons; correlated errors across seasons are unmodelled Fixed-effects panel model or clustered standard errors at the team level
Top divisions only Results may not generalise to lower divisions or smaller national leagues Replicate in Eredivisie, Championship, Scottish Premiership
NBA comparison is a single, small sample The NBA cross-sport comparison above rests on only 300 team-seasons and 10 champion-seasons; NBA titles are also playoff-determined, not table position, making the sample smaller and structurally different from the football data Extend to further capped/uncapped leagues (NFL, MLB, EuroLeague basketball) for a larger cross-sport sample

12 Technical Appendix

12.1 A. Full Coefficient Table (All Models A–E)

coef_all <- read_csv("outputs/08_regression_results.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

coef_all |>
  filter(grepl("^[ABCDE]", model_label)) |>
  select(model_label, term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  mutate(
    across(c(estimate, std.error, statistic), ~ round(., 4)),
    p_display = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001 ***",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ sprintf("%.4f **", p.value),
      p.value < 0.05  ~ sprintf("%.4f *",  p.value),
      TRUE            ~ sprintf("%.4f",    p.value)
    ),
    model_label = sub(":.*", "", model_label)
  ) |>
  select(-p.value) |>
  kbl(longtable = TRUE,
    col.names = c("Model", "Term", "Estimate", "Std. Error",
                  "t / z", "p-value"),
    caption   = "Table A1. Full regression coefficient table (Models A–E)."
  ) |>
  kable_styling(
    latex_options = c("repeat_header", "striped"),
    full_width    = FALSE
  ) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white") |>
  collapse_rows(columns = 1, valign = "top")
Table A1. Full regression coefficient table (Models A–E).

LeaguePremier League 0.1922 0.3402 0.5650 0.5722

Financial_Rank -1.2985 0.1900 -6.8343 < 0.001 ***

12.2 B. Model H Quadratic Coefficients (Detailed)

coef_H_raw |>
  mutate(
    term  = recode(term,
      "(Intercept)"         = "Intercept",
      "Financial_Rank"      = "Financial Rank (b1)",
      "I(Financial_Rank^2)" = "FR-squared (b2)"
    ),
    across(c(estimate, std.error, statistic, conf.low, conf.high), ~ round(., 5)),
    p.value = formatC(p.value, format = "e", digits = 3)
  ) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, conf.low, conf.high) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Term", "Estimate", "SE", "t-value", "p-value",
                  "CI lower", "CI upper"),
    caption   = "Table A2. Model H (Position ~ FR + FR-sq) full coefficient table with 95pct CIs."
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white")
Table A2. Model H (Position ~ FR + FR-sq) full coefficient table with 95pct CIs.
Term Estimate SE t-value p-value CI lower CI upper
Intercept 0.4172 0.3441 1.212 2.257e-01 -0.2573 1.0916
Financial Rank (b1) 1.2309 0.0765 16.093 7.648e-52 1.0810 1.3808
FR-squared (b2) -0.0203 0.0036 -5.633 2.323e-08 -0.0273 -0.0132

12.3 C. R Code Used in This Analysis

The complete R scripts used to produce all data, models, and outputs are available in the project directory. Below we reproduce the key analysis steps for transparency and reproducibility.

Data loading and model setup:

# Load and clean the master dataset (corrected: SPAL 2017-18 market value fix)
df <- read_csv("data/merged/master_dataset_corrected.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  filter(!is.na(League_Position), !is.na(Financial_Rank),
         !is.na(Market_Value_M_EUR), !is.na(Log_Normalized_Value)) |>
  mutate(
    League       = factor(as.character(League), levels = league_order),
    Champion_bin = as.integer(
      tolower(as.character(Champion)) %in% c("yes", "true", "1")
    )
  )

Fitting the regression models:

# OLS models
modA <- lm(League_Position ~ Financial_Rank,                      data = df)
modB <- lm(League_Position ~ Financial_Rank + League,             data = df)
modC <- lm(League_Position ~ Log_Normalized_Value,                data = df)
modD <- lm(League_Position ~ Log_Normalized_Value + League + Season_Index, data = df)
modH <- lm(League_Position ~ Financial_Rank + I(Financial_Rank^2), data = df)

# Logistic regression for championship probability
modE <- glm(Champion_bin ~ Financial_Rank, data = df, family = binomial())

Computing residuals and outlier flags:

aug_H <- augment(modH, data = df) |>
  rename(Fitted_H = .fitted, Residual_H = .resid, CooksD = .cooksd)

resid_sd <- sd(aug_H$Residual_H, na.rm = TRUE)

aug_H <- aug_H |>
  mutate(
    Outlier_Flag = case_when(
      Residual_H < -1.5 * resid_sd ~ "Overperformer",
      Residual_H >  1.5 * resid_sd ~ "Underperformer",
      TRUE                          ~ "Normal"
    )
  )

Model comparison using AIC:

# Compare all OLS models by AIC, R-squared, and adjusted R-squared
model_comparison <- list(A = modA, B = modB, C = modC, D = modD, H = modH) |>
  map_dfr(glance, .id = "Model") |>
  transmute(Model, R2 = round(r.squared, 4),
            Adj_R2 = round(adj.r.squared, 4),
            AIC    = round(AIC, 1),
            BIC    = round(BIC, 1))

Key analysis scripts in the project:

Script Purpose
RScripts/01_scrape_ESPN_standings.R Download league standings from ESPN
RScripts/02_scrape_transfermarkt_market_values.R Download squad market values
RScripts/03_compute_financial_rank.R Assign within-league Financial Rank
RScripts/04_merge_standings_and_market_values.R Build master dataset
RScripts/05_analysis_and_regression.R Core OLS and logit models
RScripts/05b_extended_regression.R Robustness checks and extended models
RScripts/05c_model_h4_extended.R Model H4: League + Season extensions (Section 9.4)
RScripts/06_residuals_and_outliers.R Residual analysis, Cook’s Distance
RScripts/07_beautiful_charts.R Publication-quality visualisations

R version 4.5.3
Platform Windows 11 x64 (build 26200)
Date knitted 2026-07-09
Key packages dplyr, ggplot2, broom, kableExtra, ggrepel

Report generated with R Markdown. Data sources: ESPN FC (league standings), Transfermarkt (squad market values). All analysis scripts are available in the project repository. For questions, contact: mdmuktadir0611@gmail.com
